title: Limits at infinity: graphical
description: Match expressions for limits at infinity with graphical behavior.
topic_path: Connecting limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider graphs A, B, and C. The dashed lines represent asymptotes.

**Which graphs agree with this statement?**

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -\infty}f(x)=2$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: As $x$ approaches negative infinity, what value does $f(x)$ seem to approach on each of the graphs?
Only on graphs B and C does $f(x)$ seem to approach $2$ as $x$ approaches negative infinity.

choices.content: ![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 4 to 4. The graph consists of 3 curves. The first curve starts at about (negative 4, 2), then moves downward toward a vertical asymptote at x = negative 2. The second curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = negative 2, through (0, 2), and toward a vertical asymptote at x = 2. The third curve moves upward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 2, to about (4, 2).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/68d46200c2859b4d3565831add0ab8904dd1fad3)

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 4 to 4. The graph consists of 3 curves. The first curve moves downward away from a horizontal asymptote at y = 2, then toward a vertical asymptote at x = 0. The second curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 0 to about (1, 3), then moves upward toward a vertical asymptote at x = 2. The third curve moves upward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 2, then toward the horizontal asymptote at y = 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/444544ff2a8539027fd6cfff3af5e71fea6f5249)

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 4 to 4. The graph consists of 2 curves. The first curve moves downward away from a horizontal asymptote at y = 2, then toward a vertical asymptote at x = 2. The second curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 2, then toward a horizontal asymptote at y = negative 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/625d8719662d13e91ca2a5595c62a0e2d6e94cbf)

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider graphs A, B, and C. The dashed lines represent asymptotes.

**Which graphs agree with this statement?**

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to \infty}h(x)=0$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: As $x$ approaches positive infinity, what value does $h(x)$ seem to approach on each of the graphs?
Only on graph B does $h(x)$ seem to approach $0$ as $x$ approaches positive infinity.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 4 to 4. The graph consists of 2 curves. assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the graph of function $h$. The dashed lines represent asymptotes.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 2 curves. The first curve moves downward away from a horizontal asymptote at y = 2, then toward a vertical asymptote at x = 3. The second curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 3, then toward a horizontal asymptote at y = negative 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/604ae4a4eca1425e496ce734842898410e9ea76a)

**Find the limits of $h$ at negative and positive infinity.**

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-\infty}h(x)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to \infty}h(x)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-\infty}h(x)$ represents the value $h$ approaches as $x$ becomes infinitely negative (i.e., as we move on the graph to the left).

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}h(x)$ represents the value $h$ approaches as $x$ becomes infinitely positive (i.e., as we move on the graph to the right).

![The graph of function h has the first curve approaching  the horizontal asymptote y = 2 as x approaches negative infinity. The second curve approaches the horizontal asymptote y = negative 2 as x approaches positive infinity.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2474bf49f113607071170221a341b729814e78be)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-\infty}h(x)$ seems to approach $2$ and $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}h(x)$ seems to approach $-2$.

In conclusion, these are the limits of $h$ at negative and positive infinity:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-\infty}h(x)=2
\\\\
&\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}h(x)=-2
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the graph of function $f$. The dashed lines represent asymptotes.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves. The first curve moves downward away from a horizontal asymptote at y = 0, then toward a vertical asymptote at x = negative 2. The second curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = negative 2 to about (0, 2.2), then moves upward toward a vertical asymptote at x = 2. The third curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 2, then toward a horizontal asymptote at y = negative 4.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d7b3120fa09807d9a17c7ab36cdd61495c04a153)

**Find the limits of $f$ at negative and positive infinity.**

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-\infty}f(x)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to \infty}f(x)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-\infty}f(x)$ represents the value $f$ approaches as $x$ becomes infinitely negative (i.e., as we move on the graph to the left).

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}f(x)$ represents the value $f$ approaches as $x$ becomes infinitely positive (i.e., as we move on the graph to the right).

![The graph of function f has the first curve approaching the horizontal asymptote y = 0 as x approaches negative infinity. The third curve approaches the horizontal asymptote y = negative 4 as x approaches positive infinity.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5408350174670b613aebd8f8859f097dfd17caf3)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-\infty}f(x)$ seems to approach $0$ and $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}f(x)$ seems to approach $-4$.

In conclusion, these are the limits of $f$ at negative and positive infinity:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-\infty}f(x)=0
\\\\
&\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}f(x)=-4
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the graph of function $g$. The dashed lines represent asymptotes.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves. The first curve moves downward away from a horizontal asymptote at y = negative 3, then toward a vertical asymptote at x = negative 4. The second curve moves upward away from the vertical asymptote at x = negative 4 to about (negative 2.5, negative 0.5), then moves downward toward a vertical asymptote at x = negative 1. The third curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = negative 1, then toward a horizontal asymptote at y = 0.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6a8fe703af0e4ad3788fc97624a33c7f8819500f)

**Find the limits of $g$ at negative and positive infinity.**

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-\infty}g(x)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to \infty}g(x)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-\infty}g(x)$ represents the value $g$ approaches as $x$ becomes infinitely negative (i.e., as we move on the graph to the left).

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}g(x)$ represents the value $g$ approaches as $x$ becomes infinitely positive (i.e., as we move on the graph to the right).

![The graph of function g has the first curve approaching the horizontal asymptote y = negative 3 as x approaches negative infinity. The second curve approaches the horizontal asymptote y = 0 as x approaches positive infinity.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8b7c848a290d98d08ed17138c6e379453dffbbc4)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-\infty}g(x)$ seems to approach $-3$ and $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}g(x)$ seems to approach $0$.

In conclusion, these are the limits of $g$ at negative and positive infinity:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-\infty}g(x)=-3
\\\\
&\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}g(x)=0
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the graph of function $h$. The dashed lines represent asymptotes.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves. The first curve moves downward away from a horizontal asymptote at y = negative 1, then toward a vertical asymptote at x = 0. The second curve moves upward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 0 to about (1.5, 1.5), then moves downward toward a vertical asymptote at x = 3. The third curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 3, then toward a horizontal asymptote at y = 5.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2672ace0e79718d3ab91889593c1c6862471938c)

**Find the limits of $h$ at negative and positive infinity.**

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-\infty}h(x)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to \infty}h(x)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-\infty}h(x)$ represents the value $h$ approaches as $x$ becomes infinitely negative (i.e., as we move on the graph to the left).

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}h(x)$ represents the value $h$ approaches as $x$ becomes infinitely positive (i.e., as we move on the graph to the right).

![The graph of function h has the first curve approaching the 
 horizontal asymptote y = negative 1 as x approaches negative infinity. The third curve approaches  the horizontal asymptote y = 5 as x approaches positive infinity.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/eeb485c49081e41ad75c25bcf7da047898055e42)

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-\infty}h(x)$ seems to approach $-1$ and $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}h(x)$ seems to approach $5$.

In conclusion, these are the limits of $h$ at negative and positive infinity:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-\infty}h(x)=-1
\\\\
&\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}h(x)=5
\end{align}$

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}f(x)$ represents the value $f$ approaches as $x$ becomes infinitely positive (i.e., as we move on the graph to the right).
